# Degradation of lignin in different lignocellulosic biomass by steam explosion combined with microbial consortium treatment

[*Biotechnology for Biofuels and Bioproducts*](/)
**volume 16**, Article number: 55 (2023)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

6483 Accesses

32 Citations

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2/metrics)

## Abstract

The difficulty of degrading lignin is the main factor limiting the high-value conversion process of lignocellulosic biomass. The biodegradation of lignin has attracted much attention because of its strong environmental friendliness, but it still faces some dilemmas such as slow degradation rate and poor adaptability. The microbial consortia with high lignin degradation efficiency and strong environmental adaptability were obtained in our previous research. To further increase the lignin degradation efficiency, this paper proposes a composite treatment technology of steam explosion combined with microbial consortium degradation to treat three kinds of biomass. We measured the lignin degradation efficiency, selectivity value (SV) and enzymatic saccharification efficiency. The structural changes of the biomass materials and microbial consortium structure were also investigated. The experimental results showed that after 1.6 MPa steam explosion treatment, the lignin degradation efficiency of the eucalyptus root reached 35.35% on the 7th days by microbial consortium. At the same time, the lignin degradation efficiency of the bagasse and corn straw treated by steam explosion followed by microbial biotreatment was 37.61–44.24%, respectively, after only 7 days of biotreatment. The microbial consortium also showed strong selectivity degradation to lignin. The composite treatment technology can significantly improve the enzymatic saccharification efficiency. Saccharomycetales, *Ralstonia* and Pseudomonadaceae were the dominant microorganisms in the biomass degradation systems. It was proved that the combined treatment technology of steam explosion and microbial consortium degradation could overcome the drawbacks of traditional microbial pretreatment technology, and can facilitate the subsequent high-value conversion of lignocellulose.

## Introduction

Due to the large consumption of fossil energy in the world, excessive greenhouse gases are emitted into the air every year. The environmental harm and climate impact caused by the increase of greenhouse gas content have been widely concerned all over the world. In 2020, China’s total carbon emission reached 10.251 billion tons, with the energy consumption and carbon dioxide emissions second only to the United States [[1](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR1 "Chi Y, Zhou W, Tang S, Hu Y. Driving factors of CO2 emissions in China’s power industry: relative importance analysis based on spatial durbin model. Energies. 2022;15(7):1–15. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/en15072631
                  
                .")]. The carbon dioxide emission of China would rise to the first around 2025 [[2](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR2 "Yu S, Zheng S, Li X, Li L. China can peak its energy-related carbon emissions before 2025: evidence from industry restructuring. Energ Econ. 2018;73:91–107. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eneco.2018.05.012
                  
                .")]. Therefore, it is urgently needed to control carbon dioxide emissions and reduce the use of fossil energy at present. Many countries in the world are implementing carbon emission reduction plans. For example, China has proposed the *Dual Carbon* strategic goal of achieving carbon peak by 2030 and carbon neutrality by 2060. On the other hand, with the tension and changes in the international situation, there is a great risk of countries relying too much on fossil energy. Therefore, the efficient use of biomass energy has received extensive attention.

The efficient utilization of biomass energy can replace the consumption of some fossil energy and reduce carbon emissions [[3](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR3 "Field CB, Campbell JE, Lobell DB. Biomass energy: the scale of the potential resource. Trends Ecol Evol. 2008;23(2):65–72. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tree.2007.12.001
                  
                .")]. Biomass will absorb carbon dioxide in the atmosphere during its growth, and biomass energy is a carbon *Zero emission* energy in the sense of life cycle analysis. Lignocellulose biomass is the most abundant renewable biomass resource on earth, with wide sources and low cost. According to the data from the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs of China, the production of straw lignocellulose in China reached 797 million tons in 2020 [[4](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR4 "Jin H, Xu C, Yang L, Qu G, Peng J. High value utilization strategy of agricultural waste straw. South China Agric. 2021;15(23):219–20. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1941/j.cnki.1673-890x.2021.23.101
                  
                .")]. If effective treatment technology can be developed to convert the waste lignocellulose biomass into high-value products such as biofuel ethanol, the shortage of fossil energy can be effectively alleviated, and the carbon emission caused by lignocellulose incineration should be reduced [[5](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR5 "Chen J, Zhang B, Luo L, Zhang F, Yi Y, Shan Y, Liu B, Zhou Y, Wang X, Lü X. A review on recycling techniques for bioethanol production from lignocellulosic biomass. Renew Sust Energ Rev. 2021;149:111370. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rser.2021.111370
                  
                .")]. Therefore, it is vital to develop and utilize lignocellulose biomass resources.

The raw materials of lignocellulose are mainly composed of cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin. Lignin is closely connected with hemicellulose molecules through covalent and hydrogen bonds, making lignocellulose a strong structure and highly resistant to enzymatic and microbial hydrolysis [[6](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR6 "Veluchamy C, Kalamdhad AS. Influence of pretreatment techniques on anaerobic digestion of pulp and paper mill sludge: a review. Bioresour Technol. 2017;245:1206–19. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2017.08.179
                  
                .")]. Lignin is considered as a natural barrier against the degradation of lignocellulose biomass, which brings difficulties to the industrial biorefinery of lignocellulose biomass. The degradation of lignin can improve the pretreatment efficiency of lignocellulosic biomass, realize the effective separation of cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin, and mildly convert lignocellulosic biomass into bioenergy and other valuable products [[7](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR7 "Sindhu R, Binod P, Pandey A. Biological pretreatment of lignocellulosic biomass–an overview. Bioresour Technol. 2016;199:76–82. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2015.08.030
                  
                .")]. Exploring and developing efficient lignin degradation technology has become a research hotspot in recent years [[8](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR8 "Bugg TDH, Williamson JJ, Rashid GMM. Bacterial enzymes for lignin depolymerisation: new biocatalysts for generation of renewable chemicals from biomass. Curr Opin Biotech. 2020;55:26–33. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cbpa.2019.11.007
                  
                .")].

The existing research on the efficient degradation of lignin indicates that lignin can be degraded by physical and chemical methods. However, most of these methods require harsh operating conditions or high energy-consumption equipment, and there are some problems such as inhibitors in the degradation process, affecting the subsequent enzymatic hydrolysis and fermentation process. Biological pretreatment is considered as a potential method because of its advantages of low energy consumption, little chemical addition and environmental friendliness [[9](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR9 "Ragauskas AJ, Beckham GT, Biddy MJ, Chandra R, Chen F, Davis MF, Davison BH, Dixon RA, Gilna P, Keller M, Langan P, Naskar AK, Saddler JN, Tschaplinski TJ, Tuskan GA, Wyman CE. Lignin valorization: improving lignin processing in the biorefinery. Science. 2014;344(6185):1246843. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1126/science.124684
                  
                .")].

Traditional lignin-biodegradation microorganisms are represented by fungi such as white rot fungi, but fungal degradation faces some problems, such as slow reaction process and high requirements for strain culture environment and conditions [[10](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR10 "Chen Z, Wan CX. Biological valorization strategies for converting lignin into fuels and chemicals. Renew Sust Energy Rev. 2017;73:610–21. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rser.2017.01.166
                  
                .")]. Compared with fungi, bacteria can degrade lignin in a faster way, but its degradation efficiency is lower [[11](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR11 "Xu WY, Fu SF, Yang ZM, Lu J, Guo RB. Improved methane production from corn straw by microaerobic pretreatment with a pure bacteria system. Bioresour Technol. 2018;259:18–23. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2018.02.046
                  
                .")]. The direct use of lignin-degrading enzymes has the disadvantages of high cost and easy inactivation. How to achieve rapid, efficient and low-cost degradation is the key problem to be solved in the industrial application of lignin microbial degradation.

The decomposition of lignin in nature is the result of the cooperation of fungi and bacteria in the natural microbial community [[12](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR12 "Wilhelm RC, Singh R, Eltis LD, Mohn WW. Bacterial contributions to delignification and lignocellulose degradation in forest soils with metagenomic and quantitative stable isotope probing. ISME J. 2019;13:413–29. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41396-018-0279-6
                  
                .")]. In view of the potential strengths of microbial consortium, our previous research successfully screened four microbial consortia of J-1, J-6, J-8 and J-15 with high lignin degradation efficiency from the decayed wooden relics [[13](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR13 "Zhang W, Ren X, Lei Q, Wang L. Screening and comparison of lignin degradation microbial consortia from wooden antiques. Molecules. 2021;26:2862. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/molecules26102862
                  
                .")]. Among them, the lignin degradation efficiency of J-6 was highest and reached 54% after 48 h treatment with an initial lignin concentration of 0.5 g/L. Our work also demonstrated the potential utilization of microbial consortia via the synergy of fungi and bacteria, which can overcome the shortcomings of traditional lignin biodegradation when using a single strain. This indicates the potential application of the microbial consortium to lignin degradation and removal. In addition, microorganisms in the microbial consortium can form metabolic complementarity or nutrition dependence through interaction in the metabolic system, thereby driving the metabolic division of labor. As a result, the microbial consortium always has a high tolerance to nutritional shocks and shows adaptability to different environments [[14](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR14 "Ma XC, Li XK, Wang XW, Liu GG, Zuo JL, Wang ST, Wang K. Impact of salinity on anaerobic microbial community structure in high organic loading purified terephthalic acid wastewater treatment system. J Hazard Mater. 2020;383:121132. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2019.121132
                  
                .")]. The microbial consortia screened in our previous research presented high degradation efficiency in the process of degrading alkali lignin (a kind of purified lignin commodity). So, it is of great necessity to explore the application of these lignin degrading microbial consortia in the biomass treatment. Of course, other researchers report some similar studies. For example, Fang et al. screened the microbial consortium from the decaying plant for the treatment of tree trimmings biomass, which can realize the selective degradation of lignin, and this microbial consortium was also composed of fungi and bacteria [[15](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR15 "Fang X, Li Q, Lin Y, Lin X, Dai Y, Guo Z, Pan D. Screening of a microbial consortium for selective degradation of lignin from tree trimmings. Bioresour Technol. 2018;254:247–55. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2018.01.058
                  
                .")]. Ali et al. investigated the effective bio-pretreatment of sawdust waste utilizing a novel microbial consortium and reported that the capability of a single strain to degrade lignin was much weaker than using a microbial consortium [[16](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR16 "Ali SS, Abomohra AE, Sun JZ. Effective bio-pretreatment of sawdust waste with a novel microbial consortium for enhanced biomethanation. Bioresour Technol. 2017;238:425–32. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2017.03.187
                  
                .")].

On the other hand, according to the analysis of the existing research results, compared with other physical and chemical methods, biological pretreatment still has dilemmas in low lignin degradation efficiency and long degradation time, which is not conducive to industrial application. Previous research has shown that it is a promising method to combine biological pretreatment with other pretreatment methods to achieve better treatment effect. Consequently, this research selected microbial consortia with high lignin degradation rate and strong environment adaptability to degrade lignin in three kinds of biomass (eucalyptus root, bagasse and corn straw) and biodegradation process was combined with other physical–chemical method to make up for the shortcomings. The degradation mechanism of lignin can be better explained by analyzing and comparing the degradation of different biomass. The research objects mainly include the degradation efficiency of lignin by the microbial consortium in different biomass under different physical–chemical pretreatment conditions such as steam explosion treatment, the selective degradation ability of the microbial consortium and the cellulase hydrolysis efficiency after the microbial consortium treatment. Meanwhile, the degradation mechanism was discussed and analyzed by examining the changes of morphology and community structure in the process of biomass degradation. The research results aim to provide reference for the efficient utilization of microbial consortium to degrade lignocellulose.

## Materials and methods

### Materials

The original microbial consortia were screened from the wooden relics [[13](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR13 "Zhang W, Ren X, Lei Q, Wang L. Screening and comparison of lignin degradation microbial consortia from wooden antiques. Molecules. 2021;26:2862. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/molecules26102862
                  
                .")]. Through the preliminary biomass degradation experiment, two microbial consortia J-6 and J-1 with the highest lignin degradation efficiency were selected for this research. According to genomic sequencing analyses, the fungi of J-6 were dominated by Saccharomycetales (98.92%) and the bacteria of J-6 were mainly *Shinella* sp. (47.38%), *Cupriavidus* sp. (29.84%), and *Bosea* sp. (7.96%) and the fungi of J-1 were dominated by Saccharomycetales (98.56%) and the bacteria of J-1 were mainly *Serratia* sp. (95.23%) *and Yersinia* sp. (2.33%) [[13](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR13 "Zhang W, Ren X, Lei Q, Wang L. Screening and comparison of lignin degradation microbial consortia from wooden antiques. Molecules. 2021;26:2862. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/molecules26102862
                  
                .")]. Species with low relative abundance were not listed. In this experiment, eucalyptus root (Guangxi, China), bagasse (Guangxi, China) and corn straw (Heilongjiang, China), which are common lignocellulosic biomass in China, were selected for the experiments. The content of biomass components is as follows. For eucalyptus root, the lignin content was 26.31 ± 2.94%, cellulose was 40.48 ± 1.95%, and hemicellulose was 18.80 ± 1.54%. For bagasse, the lignin content was 20.76 ± 2.73%, cellulose was 42.95 ± 4.42%, and hemicellulose was 20.82 ± 0.94%. For corn straw, the lignin content was 16.37 ± 3.69%, cellulose was 39.54 ± 5.43%, and hemicellulose was 24.91 ± 4.80%. The biomass materials were screened with a 40–80 mesh sieve after crushing, and dried at 105 °C in an oven for 72 h to prepare for bio-degradation experiments. The biological reagent used in the experiment was purchased from Sigma Company. Other reagents (analytical grade) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Industry Group Co., Ltd.

### Culture conditions

#### Domestication medium

The domestication medium was composed of 2.5 g/L peptone, 1 g/L potassium dihydrogen phosphate, 2.5 g/L sodium chloride, 0.2 g/L MgSO4·7H2O, 0.1 g/L calcium chloride, 1.6 g/L untreated raw biomass (eucalyptus root, bagasse or corn straw) and 1 mL/L trace element solution. Trace element solution included 0.1 g/L ZnSO4 7H2O, 0.1 g/L MnSO4·H2O, 0.15 g/L CuSO4·5H2O, 0.16 g/L CoCl2·6H2O, 0.8 g/L Na2MoO4 8H2O, 0.02 g/L H3BO3, and 0.05 g/L NiCl2·6H2O.

#### Basic medium

The composition of culture medium was as follows: 2.5 g/L (NH4)2SO4, 1 g/L potassium dihydrogen phosphate, 2.5 g/L sodium chloride, 0.2 g/L MgSO4·7H2O, 0.1 g/L calcium chloride, 0.5 g/L glucose and 1 mL/L trace elements, which were obtained from our previous experiments. Besides, 1 mol/L HCl or NaOH solution was added to the solution to adjust the pH value.

#### Degradation medium

Firstly, 1.6 g/L of biomass (eucalyptus root, bagasse and corn straw) was added to the basic medium to prepare the biomass degradation medium. At the same time, to degrade biomass better, the biomass was pretreated by steam explosion method. Therefore, the biomass under four different treatment conditions (no steam explosion treatment, 1.2 MPa, 1.6 MPa and 2.0 MPa treatment) was added to the basic medium with the same concentration. The concentration of biomass was obtained by optimizing the lignin degradation experiment.

### Biomass pretreatment method

Before steam explosion treatment, the biomass was firstly immersed in water for 10 min, and then the steam explosion equipment (QB-200, Zhengdao Machine Factory, Henan, China) was used for steam pretreatment at the pressure of 1.2 MPa, 1.6 MPa and 2.0 MPa, with the pressure held for 180 s.

### Lignin degradation experiments

The original microbial consortium was transferred to the domestication medium with 10% inoculum volume. Three different domestication media were prepared by three kinds of biomass to acclimate and culture the microbial consortia, respectively. The microbial consortia were cultivated at pH 5, 30 °C and 160 rpm for 14 days for domestication. Then the microbial consortia were cultured in the degradation medium for three cycles, followed by the lignin degradation optimization experiments. The lignin degradation experiment was conducted by changing the culture conditions. By changing the culture conditions, the optimization experiment of lignin degradation in biomass was carried out. In the control group, no microorganisms were added under the same operation mode. The culture conditions changed in the lignin degradation optimization experiment include carbon source (different biomass and different steam explosion pretreatment), pH (4–8) and degradation time (3–28 days). Through the degradation optimization experiment, the best lignin degradation efficiency was obtained and the factors affecting lignin degradation were determined. The degradation medium was placed in a constant-temperature shaker (HS-211B, Shanghai Huanjing Test Equipment Factory), incubated at a temperature of 30 °C and a rotating speed of 180 rpm. After the degradation reaction, the residual biomass was tested and analyzed to judge the degradation of different components (mainly including lignin, cellulose and hemicellulose).

### Analytical methods

#### Determination of three components

The contents of lignin, cellulose and hemicellulose components in biomass were determined according to the NREL method of the U.S. Department of Energy [[17](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR17 "Sluiter A, Hames B, Ruiz R, Scarlata C, Sluiter J, Templeton D, Crocker D. Determination of
Structural Carbohydrates and Lignin in Biomass,Technical Report NREL/TP-510-42618. Contract No. DE-AC36-08-
GO28308. The National Renew. Energy Lab. (NREL) of the US Dep. of Energy (USDOE), Golden CO. 2012. 
                  https://www.nrel.gov/docs/gen/fy13/42618.pdf
                  
                ")]. The biomass dried to constant weight was extracted with the extractor (APLE-3500, Beijing Jitian Equipment Co., Ltd., China). Ultra-pure water and absolute ethanol were selected as the extraction solvents. The extraction temperature was 100 °C, and the rinsing volume was 40% (the volume of the extraction tank was 34 mL). The heat balance and extraction time was 300 s with a final purging process lasting for 60 s. The samples were transferred to glass dishes after extraction, and dried in the oven again. The extracted biomass was then treated by acidolysis, filtration, drying, weighing and other steps according to the standard method [[17](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR17 "Sluiter A, Hames B, Ruiz R, Scarlata C, Sluiter J, Templeton D, Crocker D. Determination of
Structural Carbohydrates and Lignin in Biomass,Technical Report NREL/TP-510-42618. Contract No. DE-AC36-08-
GO28308. The National Renew. Energy Lab. (NREL) of the US Dep. of Energy (USDOE), Golden CO. 2012. 
                  https://www.nrel.gov/docs/gen/fy13/42618.pdf
                  
                ")]. The sugar concentration was determined by high performance liquid chromatograph (HPLC, LC-40D, Shimadzu, Japan) to calculate the content of cellulose and hemicellulose components. The SV was introduced to characterize the selective degradation efficiency of lignin during the biomass degradation process by microbial consortia [[18](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR18 "Mishra V, Jana AK, Jana MM, Gupta A. Improvement of selective lignin degradation in fungal pretreatment of sweet sorghum bagasse using synergistic CuSO4-syringic acid supplements. J Environ Manage. 2017;193:558–66. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2017.02.057
                  
                .")].

#### Calculation of degradation efficiency

The degradation efficiency of the three components can be calculated by: Degradation efficiency (%) = (initial content \* initial biomass mass—content after treatment \* biomass mass after treatment)/initial content \* initial biomass mass.

And according to NERL method [[17](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR17 "Sluiter A, Hames B, Ruiz R, Scarlata C, Sluiter J, Templeton D, Crocker D. Determination of
Structural Carbohydrates and Lignin in Biomass,Technical Report NREL/TP-510-42618. Contract No. DE-AC36-08-
GO28308. The National Renew. Energy Lab. (NREL) of the US Dep. of Energy (USDOE), Golden CO. 2012. 
                  https://www.nrel.gov/docs/gen/fy13/42618.pdf
                  
                ")], the three components content can be calculated by:

Lignin content (%) = acid soluble lignin content (%) + acid insoluble lignin content (%).where *C*c-6 (g/L) is the hexoses (glucose, galactose, and mannose) concentration by acid hydrolysis and *C*c-5 (g/L) is the pentoses (xylose and arabinose) concentration by acid hydrolysis.

SV = lignin degradation efficiency/cellulose degradation efficiency.

#### Determination of saccharification efficiency

According to the literature [[19](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR19 "Liang J, Fang X, Lin Y, Wang D. A new screened microbial consortium OEM2 for lignocellulosic biomass deconstruction and chlorophenols detoxification. J Hazard Mater. 2018;347:341–8. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2018.01.023
                  
                .")], 20 FPU/g of cellulase (CAS: 9012-54-8) was added to assay the saccharification efficiency of the biomass before and after the lignin degradation treatment. After the saccharification reaction, samples were centrifugated with high-speed centrifuge (Sorvall ST8, ThermoFisher) at 5000 rpm for 3 min, and the reducing sugar content was determined simultaneously by HPLC and DNS method. The saccharification efficiency was calculated as follows: saccharification efficiency (or the yield of enzymatic hydrolysis) % = Mass of reducing sugar produced \*0.9/mass of cellulose and hemicellulose in the sample.

#### Morphological detection

The microstructure of the biomass samples was characterized by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, SUPRA55, Carl Zeiss AG, Germany). The biomass samples were treated into fine filaments and then glued to the SEM sample holder. After spraying gold, the morphological changes of biomass were analyzed at an accelerating voltage of 5.0 kV. The fiber crystallinity test was conducted using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD-6000, Shimadzu, Japan) according to the previous research [[18](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR18 "Mishra V, Jana AK, Jana MM, Gupta A. Improvement of selective lignin degradation in fungal pretreatment of sweet sorghum bagasse using synergistic CuSO4-syringic acid supplements. J Environ Manage. 2017;193:558–66. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2017.02.057
                  
                .")].

#### Community structure detection

To ensure the uniformity of results, the kits and primer pairs used in this study were the same as those in our previous study [[13](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR13 "Zhang W, Ren X, Lei Q, Wang L. Screening and comparison of lignin degradation microbial consortia from wooden antiques. Molecules. 2021;26:2862. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/molecules26102862
                  
                .")]. Microbial community genomic DNA was extracted from the samples using the E.Z.N.A.® soil DNA Kit (Omega Bio-Tek, USA). The primer pair 515F (5ʹ-GTGCCAGCMGCCGCGG-3ʹ) and 806R (5ʹ-GGACTACHVGGGTWTCTAAT-3ʹ) was used to amplify bacterial 16S rRNA genes in the community. The primer pair ITS1F (5ʹ-CTTGGTCATTTAGAGGAAGTAA-3ʹ) and ITS2R (5ʹ- GCTGCGTTCTTCATCGATGC-3ʹ) was used to amplify fungal ITS genes. The purified amplicons were sequenced on MiSeq PE300 platform/NovaSeq PE250 platform (Illumina, USA). The data processing method of gene sequence was based on the literature [[20](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR20 "Chen S, Zhou Y, Chen Y, Gu J. Fastp: an ultra-fast all-in-one FASTQ preprocessor. Bioinformatics. 2018;34:884–90. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1093/bioinformatics/bty560
                  
                .")].

### Statistical methods

All the above experiments were repeated 3 times and the average data were reported. One-way ANOVA was used to detect any significant differences in the results (Tukey’s test, *p* < 0.05).

## Results and discussion

### Lignin degradation in biomass

In our previous research, it has been confirmed that the microbial degradation process of lignin was mainly related to carbon source composition, degradation pH, and degradation time. Therefore, this research mainly investigates the effects of these factors on the degradation of lignin in biomass.

At the same time, our previous experiments also showed that the microorganisms of the microbial consortium had a certain demand for oxygen during the growth and the lignin utilization, because shaking incubation can achieve higher degradation efficiency. Accordingly, shaking incubation was performed in this research and the results showed that temperature had a negligible effect on lignin degradation efficiency within the range of 160–200 rpm. In addition, degradation experiments at different temperatures were also carried out. The results demonstrated that temperature also had a negligible effect on lignin degradation within the temperature range of 25–35 °C. Because the rotation speed and temperature both had little effect on the lignin degradation, in the subsequent experiments, the degradation temperature was 30 °C, and the rotation speed was 180 r/min. And lignin was not degraded in the control group without adding microorganisms.

#### Effect of pH on microbial consortium degradation

When lignin is degraded by microorganisms, the pH value of the culture medium is an important factor affecting the degradation efficiency. However, the most suitable pH value for lignin degradation by each microorganism is usually very different. For example, it was reported that the optimal pH value for *Aneuribacillus aneurilyticus* to degrade lignin was 7.6 [[21](/articles/10.1186/s13068-023-02306-2#ref-CR21 "Raj A, Chandra R, Reddy MMK, Purohit HJ, Kapley A. Biodegr

URL: https://www.osti.gov/servlets/purl/1477774

SUMMARY:
by H Wang · 2018 · Cited by 880 — Given its heterogeneous structure, lignin pyrolysis conversion covers a broad temperature range, mainly from 150°C to 800°C, with the highest degradation rate

FULL CONTENT:
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Bioresource Technology journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/biortech Review From lignin to valuable products–strategies, challenges, and prospects Hongliang Wanga,c, Yunqiao Pud, Arthur Ragauskasd,e, Bin Yanga,b,⁎ a Bioproducts, Sciences, and Engineering Laboratory, Department of Biological Systems Engineering, Washington State University, Richland, WA 99354, USA b Earth and Biological Sciences Directorate, Paciﬁc Northwest National Laboratory, Richland, WA 99352, USA c Center of Biomass Engineering/College of Agronomy and Biotechnology, China Agricultural University, Beijing 100193, China d Center for Bioenergy Innovation, Joint Institute of Biological Science, Biosciences Division, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge, TN, USA e Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering & Department of Forestry, Wildlife and Fisheries, Center for Renewable Carbon, University of Tennessee, Knoxville, TN, USA A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Lignin Biomass pretreatment Lignin characterization Lignin conversion Bioreﬁnery A B S T R A C T The exploration of eﬀective approaches for the valorization of lignin to valuable products attracts broad interests of a growing scientiﬁc community. By fully unlocking the potential of the world’s most abundant resource of bio-aromatics, it could improve the proﬁtability and carbon eﬃciency of the entire bioreﬁnery process, thus ac-celerate the replacement of fossil resources with bioresources in our society. The successful realization of this goal depends on the development of technologies to overcome the following challenges, including: 1) eﬃcient biomass pretreatment and lignin separation technologies that overcomes its diverse structure and complex chemistry challenges to obtain high purity lignin; 2) advanced chemical analysis for precise quantitative char-acterization of the lignin in chemical transformation processes; 3) novel approaches for conversion of biomass-derived lignin to valuable products. This review summarizes the latest cutting-edge innovations of lignin che-mical valorization with the focus on the aforementioned three key aspects.
1. Introduction The growing global population leads to an increasing demand for fuels and chemicals, resulting in many societal problems, including energy security and environmental concerns (Schutyser et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2018). To reach a sustainable development of society, bior-eﬁnery operations that employ inedible biomass for the production of valuable products (fuels, chemicals and materials) must be advanced. In this regard, lignocellulose, composed of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, is a promising feedstock for bioreﬁnery to produce biofuels and biochemicals (Rinaldi et al., 2016). Lignocellulose is a widely available, carbon-neutral, and inedible bioresource that could be regenerated on a world-wide basis in considerable quantities each year. However, most bioreﬁnery processes currently focus on the utilization of the carbo-hydrate fractions (cellulose and hemicellulose), while leaving lignin, the second most abundant terrestrial polymer, underutilized. The U.S.
Energy Security and Independence Act of 2007 mandates the devel-opment of 79 billion liters of second-generation biofuels annually by 2022. Assuming a yield of 355 L of bioethanol per dry ton of biomass, 223 million tons of biomass will be used annually, thus producing about 62 million tons of lignin. In this aspect, about 60% more lignin is generated than what is needed to meet internal energy use by its combustion (Li et al., 2015; Ragauskas et al., 2014). Thus, eﬃcient valorization of lignin into value-added chemicals or fuels is crucial to make the industrial scale bioreﬁnery plants cost-competitive (Xu et al., 2014) Lignin is the only large-volume renewable aromatic feedstock, which is found in most terrestrial plants typically in a range of 15–30% by dry weight and 40% by energy (Gillet et al., 2017; Lupoi et al., 2015). Traditionally, most large-scale industrial processes that use plant polysaccharides have burned lignin to generate the power needed for biomass conversion. However, because lignin-rich wastes are wet and lignin has a lower energy content than coal, its value in this role is limited to well under $50/dry ton. Furthermore, most cellulosic ethanol plants generate lignin in such quantities that it over addresses the en-ergy needs of this process. Hence, conversion of lignin to higher value fuels and chemicals will signiﬁcantly enhance overall bioreﬁnery competitiveness. For example, converting about 20% of the lignin in a plant that contains about 25% lignin into products valued at about $1.00/lb would generate additional revenue of about $100/ton of feedstock fed to a biological process, thereby substantially reducing the net feedstock cost to the overall process. 0 7 2 oxygen ratio (above 2:1) and has greater energy density than cellulose, which makes it a promising candidate for fuel and aromatics (e.g., phenol, benzene, toluene, and xylene) production (Laskar et al., 2013a; Schutyser et al., 2018). Although amply studies have focused on con-verting lignin to valuable products, only a few of these eﬀorts can be commercially proﬁtable because of low yields and low quality of ﬁnal products. Diﬃculties in lignin valorization is mainly attributed to the complexity and recalcitrance structure of lignin, and the high reactivity of lignin degraded fractions which are prone to condensation reactions.
Nevertheless, the valorization of lignosulphonates (i.e., cement ad-ditives, battery components, hydrocarbon fuels) (Ragauskas et al., 2014; Schutyser et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2018) provides a compelling translational research justiﬁcation.
The successful conversion of lignin to desired products should sur-mount several problems associated in biomass fractionation, including characterization of lignin and its derivatives, lignin depolymerization, and product upgrading. A good fractionation method should isolate lignin with high yield and high purity, and retain ß-O-4 bonds in the technical lignins, which would be beneﬁcial for subsequent conversion processes. Due to the recalcitrance of biomass and the variability of lignin, its extraction from the biomass matrix while retaining its native structure is a substantial challenge. Lignin isolation eﬃciency and its resulting structure can be aﬀected by various factors, including biomass sources, fractionation methods, and fractionation severity (Kim et al., 2018; Saratale et al., 2018; Si et al., 2017). The characterization of native lignin is also quite diﬃcult because of the heterogeneous and inconstant nature of lignin. Since lignin is a biopolymer with high molecular weight, a depolymerization step is usually needed before it is transformed to fuel or chemical compounds. While, unlike cellulose, which has a single repeating linkage of β-1,4 glucosidic bonds, lignin contains very complex linkages among its aromatic centers, including C–O–C bonds (β-O-4/4′, α-O-4/4′, α/γ-O-γ, 4-O-5/5′, etc.) and C–C bonds (5–5/5′, β-β, β-1, β-5, etc.) (Zakzeski et al., 2010; Zhou et al., 2011). Depolymerized lignin fractions usually are highly reactive under the depolymerization conditions, which leads to substantial un-controllable side reactions, including repolymerization or condensation to form new structures and/or char. Due to its heterogeneity and variability, direct production of well-deﬁned compounds from lignin is challenging. An upgrading process to decrease the complexity of pro-ducts is needed. In recent years, extensive research studies have been done to deal with these problems in order to unlock lignin's potential in the bioreﬁnery.
This review intends to give an overview of recent research on the conversion of lignin to value-added products (i.e. chemicals and fuels) with the focus on the following three aspects: 1) lignin separation, 2) lignin characterization, 3) lignin conversion, as depicted in Fig. 1.
Fundamental protocols and technologies involved in these three essential aspects are introduced. Latest innovations in reaction me-chanisms, catalysts design, solvent screening, and optimization of lignin valorization reaction conditions are summarized. Challenges and pro-spects are also presented.
2. Lignin extraction Eﬀective extraction or separation of lignin with high purity and less condensed structure from lignocellulose is crucial for lignin valorization (Hassan et al., 2018; Kim et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018a). Lignin is polymerized by a radical mechanism and it is believed that certain reactive components are covalently linked to hemicelluloses known as a lignin-carbohydrate complex (LCC), which makes lignin extraction diﬃcult (Ragauskas et al., 2014; Schutyser et al., 2018). Thus, bond rupture of LCC bonds to separate lignin from carbohydrates and partial depolymerization of lignin to make it extractable are required. The depolymerization results in breaking down lignin in biomass to smaller fractions to facilitate its solubilization from biomass. Due to the high reactivity of lignin and its fractions, repolymerization (condensation) reactions forming new C–C bonds usually accompanies lignin extrac-tion, leading to diﬀerent lignin intermediates with various properties (Schutyser et al., 2018). In general, isolated lignin varies in structure due to diﬀerent feedstocks, extraction methods, and extraction severity.
Currently, lignin in a large scale is produced from the pulp and pa-permaking industry and several bioreﬁnery processes, such as Kraft pulping, sulﬁte pulping, soda pulping, or hydrolysis/fractionation by hot water, dilute acid, alkaline, or enzymes, as listed in Table 1. The pulp and paper industry is a well-established and highly developed industry with several mature technologies. Since lignins (e.g., Kraft lignin, lignosulfonates, Soda lignin) from these technologies have been reviewed by other papers (Schutyser et al., 2018; Zakzeski et al., 2010), the current paper mainly focuses on lignin extraction from bioreﬁnery processes.
Bioreﬁnery uses biomass as a renewable resource in an attempt to replace fossil resources for the production of chemicals and fuels, re-quiring the rational utilization of all components in lignocellulose, in-cluding cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. Since individual lig-nocellulose constituents have obvious diﬀerences in physical and chemical properties, an isolation/separation process is usually needed before they could be selectively converted. The separation of lignin, hemicellulose, and cellulose from the biomass matrix is often accom-plished in a biomass pretreatment or biomass fractionation stage (Chen and Wan, 2018; Kim et al., 2018; Schutyser et al., 2018). Biomass pretreatment is the initial step of bioreﬁning targeted at reducing bio-mass recalcitrance and crucial for the downstream valorization. Various biomass pretreatment technologies have been developed, including acid hydrolysis, alkaline hydrolysis, reductive or oxidative catalytic Fig. 1. Due to diﬀerences in frac-tionation methods and fractionation severity, even the same biomass feedstock can result diﬀerently in the deligniﬁcation eﬃciency, lignin purity, and lignin structure (Li et al., 2016).
2.1. Lignin extraction via acid hydrolysis of biomass The acid hydrolysis method includes dilute acid hydrolysis pro-cesses (0–5 wt% HCl, H2SO4 or H3PO4, 120 °C–240 °C) (Liu et al., 2018b; Thomas et al., 2017) and concentrated acid hydrolysis processes (concentrated HCl, H2SO4 or HF, 18 °C–25 °C) (Shahbazi and Zhang, 2010). This method intends to depolymerize hemicellulose and cellu-lose while it also aﬀects lignin. During acid hydrolysis, low amounts of lignin could be removed in forms of soluble fragments. The soluble lignin fragments are usually highly reactive under the hydrolysis con-ditions, leading to undesired condensation reactions. The introduction of a ﬂow-through reactor design can separate lignin from the heating zone in time and restrict condensation reactions (Bhagia et al., 2016; Laskar et al., 2013b; Yan et al., 2014). The structural analysis of the ﬂow-through derived lignin showed cleavage of β-O-4 while very lim-ited lignin condensation reactions occurred. However, ﬂow-through reactor setting suﬀers from high water consumption resulting in low sugar concentration for downstream conversion. The key to solving this problem relies on the overcoming of solid loading limitation and in-creasing of mass and heat transfer. Lignin behavior has been reported in dilute acid pretreatment, namely, a) lignin droplet formation and re-deposit, b) pseudo-lignin formation, c) lignin migrating. Lignin droplets were formed at the pretreatment temperatures higher than its phase transition temperature (e.g., 120–200 °C) which caused lignin liqueﬁed and coalesced in the pretreatment liquid, and the droplets might travel in cell wall matrix and deposit back on the biomass surface (Selig et al., 2007). The droplets reportedly have higher S/G ratio than original lignin (Li et al., 2014a). Pseudo lignin is formed from carbohydrates and lignin degradation products (Sannigrahi et al., 2011).
Concentrated mineral acid hydrolysis (e.g., 72 wt% H2SO4 hydro-lysis at room temperatures) aims at converting carbohydrates in bio-mass into soluble sugars, where lignin also undergoes severe degrada-tion and repolymerization reactions. The separated lignin is highly condensed and called Klason lignin. The calculation of Klason lignin is commonly used to evaluate lignin content in biomass (Ibanez and Bauer, 2014). Due to its highly condensed structure, the selective conversion of Klason lignin to fuels and/or chemicals is diﬃcult.
2.2. Lignin extraction via alkaline hydrolysis of biomass Alkaline hydrolysis pretreatment is similar to that of alkaline pulping processes, which uses alkali (e.g. NaOH, Ca(OH)2, ammonia) to solubilize or depolymerize lignin, and make lignin extractable from biomass matrix (Kim et al., 2016). The principle diﬀerence compared to the chemical pulping process is the moderate treatment severity. Partial hydrolysis of ether linkages (e.g., β-O-4) serves to yield lignin oligo-mers, phenolic dimers, and monomers. Due to the low reaction severity, lignin is usually separated with low condensation structure. However, the deligniﬁcation eﬃciency of alkaline pretreatments is relatively low.
Ammonia or its aqueous solutions as alkaline reagents are extensively used for biomass pretreatment, with several well-known technologies, such as ammonia ﬁber explosion (AFEX) (Lau et al., 2009), anhydrous ammonia pretreatment (AAP) (Yoo et al., 2014), and ammonia recycled percolation (ARP) (Kim et al., 2009). These ammonia-induced biomass pretreatments can alter cellulose crystalline structure, break LCC bonds, and partially cleave lignin ether linkages, and thus facilitate lignin extraction from biomass matrix. The advantage of these technologies is that ammonia can be easily recovered after pretreatment due to its high volatility.
2.3. Lignin extraction via enzymatic hydrolysis of biomass Enzymatic hydrolysis of pretreated biomass uses cellulases and hemicellulases to degrade polysaccharides in biomass to soluble oligo/ monosaccharides, and meanwhile generating lignin-enriched solid re-sidues (Jiang et al., 2017; Yoo et al., 2014). The direct enzymatic hy-drolysis of biomass is hindered by the rigid and recalcitrant structure of lignocellulose, and thus an additional physical or chemical pretreat-ment is usually needed (Thomas et al., 2017). Commonly used pre-treatment methods include ball-milling, hot water pretreatment, dilute acid or alkaline hydrolysis, AFEX, and mechanical reﬁning (Schutyser et al., 2018). This step intends to reduce the recalcitrance of raw bio-mass and increase the digestibility of plant polysaccharides by enzymes, while lignin is not isolated directly at this stage. After enzymatic hy-drolysis, solid residues with relatively low lignin purity are obtained.
Since enzymatic hydrolysis is usually carried out at mild conditions, the purity and structural modiﬁcation of lignin mainly depends on the pretreatment method and its severity. To further increase lignin purity, a post-treatment, such as mild acid hydrolysis or organic solvent ex-traction, can be used to remove residual carbohydrates, protein, and/or ash in the lignin-enriched solid residue (Tolbert et al., 2014). Due to the rapid development of bioreﬁning, lignin produced from enzymatic hy-drolysis of pretreated lignocelluloses is increasing and it will be a cru-cial component of future lignin valorization in studies.
2.4. Lignin extraction via reductive or oxidative fractionation of biomass Reductive or oxidative catalytic fractionations use reductive re-agents (e.g. H2 or H2-donating solvents) or oxidants (e.g., O2, O3, H2O2) to promote the cleavage of ether linkages and LCC bonds (Galkin and Samec, 2016). These processes usually involve the use of redox cata-lysts, and in some cases, are combined with solvolytic extraction or acidic/alkali hydrolysis (Galkin et al., 2016). The isolated lignin pro-ducts from these processes are complicated and diverse in structure, including lignin oligomers along with high molecular weight compo-nents, highly depolymerized and deoxygenated lignin oil and/or lignin-derived acids. Solvolysis biomass fractionation, similar to organosolv pulping, refers to the use of organic solvents (e.g., alcohols, ethers, ketones, esters, acids) or their water-mixtures to treat biomass feed-stock, and are often in combination with redox catalysts, or mineral acids/alkalis (Chen and Wan, 2018; Kim et al., 2018; Loow et al., 2018).
Because the organic media have higher lignin solubility than water, the lignin extraction eﬃciency is high. With catalysts, lignin in an organic medium can undergo depolymerization and/or condensation reactions.
In some cases, the organic medium can react with lignin to modify lignin structures (e.g. low molecular weight alcohol solvents can etherify with lignin at the α-position of alkyl chains to generate alkoxy groups) (Lanceﬁeld et al., 2017; Renders et al., 2017). The structure of lignin from solvolysis fractionation varies depending on the organic medium employed, catalyst, and pretreatment severity.
2.5. Lignin extraction via combined pretreatment methods Though various methods have been developed, it is still a great challenge to eﬀectively fractionate lignocellulose biomass to its three individual constituents, and keep the separated lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose in good quality for their subsequent conversion. Recent research eﬀorts tend to use combined forces to deal with this challenge (Hassan et al., 2018; Kumar et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018b; Saratale et al., 2018; Wan et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018b). In this aspect, Ri-naldi et al. extensively investigated mechanocatalytic fractionation methods, which involves the use of acid catalysis and mechanical forces (milling) in a ‘one-pot process’ for the pretreatment of biomass (Calvaruso et al., 2017; Kaeldstroem et al., 2014; Kessler et al., 2018).
This technique can facilitate the conversion of biomass into water-so-luble oligosaccharides and lignin oligomers. After the subsequent acid H. Wang et al.
B i o r e s o u r c e T e c h n o l o g y x x x ( x x x x ) x x x – x x x 4 catalyzed post-hydrolysis, high yields of monosaccharides together with lignin precipitate could be obtained. Due to the mild pretreatment conditions, the structure of lignin isolated by this method is similar to that of ethanosolv lignin which has limited condensed structure. An-other alternative combined pretreatment method involving deacetyla-tion and mechanical reﬁning (DMR) was developed by Xiaowen Chen et al. in NREL (Chen et al., 2016b; Chen et al., 2014; Chen et al., 2016c).
This method relies on the use of dilute alkaline extraction of biomass to remove acetyl groups in the feedstock followed by mechanical disc reﬁning to overcome the recalcitrance of lignocellulose. The yield (over 80%) and concentration (around 150 g/L) of monomeric sugar can reach very high after enzyme catalyzed post-hydrolysis (Chen et al., 2014). Most of the lignin in biomass is solubilized under the alkali condition. After acid precipitation, solid lignin can be recovered. The isolated lignin also has less condensed structure, which is suitable for subsequent conversion. Other combined techniques, such as the use of concentrated phosphoric acid plus hydrogen peroxide (Wang et al., 2014), inorganic salts with calcium peroxide (Saratale et al., 2018) or with deep eutectic solvents (Loow et al., 2018), deep eutectic solvent with microwave (Chen and Wan, 2018), and electrolysis combined with ultra-sonication (Kumar et al., 2017), were also developed to frac-tionate biomass.
3. Lignin characterization Lignin isolation and puriﬁcation methods usually causes structural modiﬁcation on lignin macromolecular matrix. To secure native lignin used for characterization, the ideal isolation method requires low temperature, high yield, and minimum modiﬁcation on lignin structure.
Three types of isolated lignin protocols are widely used for lignin characterization: milled wood lignin (MWL) (Bjorkman, 1957), cellu-lolytic enzyme lignin (CEL) (Jiang et al., 2017), and enzymatic mild acidolysis lignin (EMAL) (Tolbert et al., 2014). Among these, dioxane/ water extracted lignin known as Bjorkman lignin or ball milled wood lignin is often considered as the representative of native lignin proto-type since it is believed to cause the least structural modiﬁcation on native lignin although the yield is less than 30% as reported (Bjorkman, 1954). CEL method is based on a ball milled lignin extraction procedure except cellulosic enzymes are added to remove carbohydrates before solvents extraction. However, the purity of the extracted lignin is still low because of the carbohydrate contamination although the yield of lignin could be up to 50% (Chang et al., 1975). To further improve the lignin purity and lignin yield, the lignin enriched solid residues from enzymatic hydrolysis are subjected to an additional mild acid hydro-lysis step to remove carbohydrate impurities and obtain EMAL. These three types of lignin isolation methods are widely used for laboratory studies and not suitable for industrial scale bioreﬁnery due to the high cost.
3.1. Molecular weights analysis Molecular weight is one of the key physicochemical properties of lignin. It can be analyzed by diﬀerent methods, including vapor pres-sure osmometry (VPO), ultraﬁltration, light scattering, mass spectro-metry, and gel permeation chromatography (GPC) (Baumberger et al., 2007; Gidh et al., 2006; Lange et al., 2016). Among these methods, GPC is widely used for lignin molecular weight analysis because of its ad-vantages: (1) broad range of molecular weights; (2) tolerance of syn-thetic and natural polymers; (3) small quantity analysis (milligram size); and (4) relatively short processing time. Weight average mole-cular weight (Mw) and number average molecular weight (Mn) of lignin can be calculated through a calibration curve established usually with polystyrene standards (Lange et al., 2016). Lignin polydispersity index, which represents the heterogeneity of lignin particle sizes, can also be calculated. For GPC analysis, derivatization of isolated lignin is usually necessary to achieve good solubility of lignin samples in organic solvents. Derivatization can be conducted by methylation, acetylation, or silylation. Acetylation using an acetic anhydride-pyridine mixture (1:1, v/v) is the most commonly employed derivatization method for lignin GPC analysis. The isolated lignin is dissolved in acetic anhydride-pyridine mixture and stirred at room temperature. After the reaction, ethanol is added into the mixture, and then evaporated using a rotary evaporator to remove solvents. This ethanol addition and evaporation is repeated until unreacted acetic acid and pyridine are completely re-moved. The acetylated lignin is dissolved in tetrahydrofuran (THF) and ﬁltered using a hydrophobic PTFE membrane ﬁlter before GPC analysis (Wang et al., 2018c). GPC has been recently used for molecular weights characterization of lignin in poplar and switchgrass during biomass pretreatment and enzymatic hydrolysis (Meng et al. 2016).
3.2. 1H NMR 1H NMR spectroscopy has long been used as a valuable technique for structural characterization of lignin (Jiang et al., 2010; Tiainen et al., 1999). This technique provides a high signal to noise (S/N) ratio in a short experimental time (typically within several minutes). How-ever, it usually suﬀers from severe signal overlapping due to its short chemical shift ranges (i.e., ∼δ 12–0 ppm) and complexity of lignin structures. 1H NMR can quantitatively examine lignin samples either as acetate derivatives or underivatized forms, providing information of some key lignin functionalities, including carboxylic acids, CH2 and CH3 groups, aliphatic and phenolic hydroxyl functionalities, β-O-4 substructures, methoxyl group, and monolignol types in lignin. Com-pared to the underivatized lignin, acetylation can provide improved spectral resolution of 1H NMR spectra of lignin sample. However, some unwanted chemical modiﬁcations may occur to the sample to some extent due to the acetylation procedure.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Lignin's complex structure and strong covalent bonds make it highly resistant to microbial and enzymatic degradation, limiting the efficiency of biomass conversion into valuable products.
Different biomass types exhibit significant variability in lignin content and structure, affecting degradation processes and necessitating tailored approaches for efficient biomass treatment.
Enhancing microbial consortia and optimizing growth conditions can improve lignin degradation rates, promoting better conversion of lignocellulosic biomass into biofuels and biochemicals.
Current pretreatment methods for biomass, such as steam explosion, face scalability challenges that hinder their application in large-scale biorefineries for efficient lignin removal.

The high costs associated with lignin degradation technologies and biomass pretreatment processes can limit their economic viability, stressing the need for cost-effective solutions to enhance competitiveness.
